result:
**Goal: Add multiplayer collaborative editing over the network — let two terminals connect peer-to-peer and co-edit the same grid in real-time, each player with a distinct cursor color.**

This is the natural next leap for the project. You've built a deeply-featured single-player experience (time-travel, clipboard, statistics, pattern I/O). The one dimension completely missing is **multiplayer**. A lightweight peer-to-peer mode using only Python's standard library (`socket`, `select`, `threading`) would let one instance host and another connect, with both users seeing each other's cursors and edits live on a shared grid. It fits the project's zero-dependency philosophy and transforms the simulator from a solo toy into a collaborative tool.
